summary: Uncle Bob argues that the human brain's computational complexity vastly exceeds anything we can currently build or simulate. He describes the brain as an analog computer of 100 billion neurons, each itself a sophisticated information processor driven by tens of thousands of digital processors (DNA machinery). The interconnections are physical, dedicated, and operate through both electrical and chemical signaling at molecular speeds. He concludes that comparing our internet infrastructure to a single brain is absurd, and artificial sentience remains far beyond our reach.
key_points: The brain is fundamentally an analog computer where information is encoded in firing rates, not discrete pulses
Each of the 100 billion neurons is itself a vastly powerful information processor, superior to a modern laptop
Neurons contain tens of thousands of digital processors (DNA/protein machinery) controlled by analog chemical signals
Cellular communication occurs through both local chemical signals and bloodstream-mediated long-distance signaling
Our entire internet infrastructure is computationally trivial compared to a single human brain
money_quotes: Your brain is a highly efficient analog computer composed of a deeply interconnected array of one hundred billion analog processors of immense computational power, each driven by tens of thousands of digital processors that are monitored and controlled through interactions both from within and without.
The raw information processing power of a single cell is vastly superior to a modern laptop.
Virtually every molecule, if not every atom, in your brain is an active component in the information processing going on between your ears.

The idea that our meager internet, which indirectly interconnects only a trifling few hundreds of millions of pitifully weak serial processors, could process the information of a single brain is absurd.
No, we will not be achieving artificial sentience any time soon.
Information is contained in the rate at which neurons fire. It is the pulse rate, not the pulses themselves, that contain and transmit information.
key_insight: The human brain's computational architecture—analog, massively parallel, and operating at molecular scales—represents a complexity so far beyond our digital systems that artificial sentience remains a distant fantasy.
